prompt:
I'm ripping out the last places where we do prices with parseFloat and toFixed — we had a checkout show $114.99 for something priced 115.00 and I never want to debug that again. All money in our system is an integer count of minor units (cents, rappen, yen). I need money.ts with three exports and a CurrencyLocale type.

CurrencyLocale describes a locale-pattern subset: symbol (can be multi-char like 'CHF'), position 'prefix' | 'suffix', symbolSpace boolean (space between symbol and number), groupSep, decimalSep, and decimals (0 for yen-style currencies).

formatMoney(minorUnits, locale) -> string. Grouping every three digits, decimals always padded to the locale width, no decimal separator at all when decimals is 0, minus sign leads the whole string ('-$1,234.56', '-1.234,56 €'). Reject anything that isn't a safe integer with a TypeError — a fractional minor-unit amount is always a bug upstream.

parseMoney(text, locale) -> minorUnits. Liberal where it's safe: surrounding whitespace, a missing symbol, missing grouping, short or missing decimal part ('$1,234' is 123400) are all fine; sign may sit before or after the symbol. Strict where it matters: empty input, characters that don't belong, a second decimal separator, or more decimal digits than the currency has must throw with a message that says what's wrong. parseMoney(formatMoney(x)) must equal x for every locale.

toMinorUnits(decimalText, decimals, mode = 'half-up') -> minorUnits. Takes the amount as a STRING and must do digit arithmetic on the string — the whole point is that '0.29' becomes exactly 29 and '123456789012.34' becomes exactly 12345678901234, which float math gets wrong. Modes: 'half-up' (half rounds away from zero), 'half-even' (exact half goes to the even neighbor, anything past a half rounds normally), 'floor' (toward negative infinity). Never return -0. Garbage strings throw.

Contract is test_money.ts (`node --test test_money.ts`), hands off the test file. Stdlib only.
